g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Translation and Scaling in 2D Computer Graphics. These are foundational transformations.
g.text: Translation and Scaling in 2D Computer Graphics

g.data-narration: Transformations allow us to move, resize, and rotate objects. They are essential for animations, games, CAD, and virtual reality applications.
g.text: Transformations are the backbone of all visual manipulations in computer graphics.
They enable animations, UI design, 3D rendering, and more.

g.data-narration: Homogeneous coordinates represent 2D points in 3D as (x, y, 1). This allows all transformations to be expressed as matrix multiplications.
g.text: In Homogeneous: point (x, y, 1) — we add a third coordinate w = 1
This enables both translation and scaling to use matrix multiplication!

g.data-narration: Translation moves every point by the same distance (tx, ty). It preserves shape, size, angles, and parallelism - a rigid body transformation.
g.text: Every point moves by (tx, ty): x' = x + tx, y' = y + ty
Preserves: parallelism, angles, shape, size. Only position changes.
Commutative: T1 × T2 = T2 × T1 (order doesn't matter)

g.data-narration: The translation matrix T(tx, ty) is a 3×3 matrix. When multiplied with [x, y, 1], it produces [x+tx, y+ty, 1].
Let's translate triangle ABC by tx=4, ty=-2. Vertex A(2,3) becomes A'(6,1), B(5,3)→B'(9,1), C(2,7)→C'(6,5).
Scaling changes object size by multiplying coordinates by (sx, sy). Uniform scaling uses equal factors; non-uniform uses different values.
g.text: Formula: x' = x × sx, y' = y × sy
Uniform: sx = sy (same in all directions) | Non-uniform: sx ≠ sy (different per axis)

g.data-narration: The scaling matrix S(sx, sy) multiplies coordinates. The identity matrix S(1,1) leaves objects unchanged.
By default, scaling occurs about the origin. To scale about a fixed point, we translate it to origin, scale, then translate back.
g.text: Default scaling is about origin (0,0). For scaling about point (xf, yf):
1. Translate so fixed point moves to origin: T(-xf, -yf)
2. Apply scaling: S(sx, sy) | 3. Translate back: T(xf, yf)

g.data-narration: The composite matrix is M = T(xf, yf) × S(sx, sy) × T(-xf, -yf). Transformations apply right-to-left: T(-xf,-yf) first, then S, then T.

g.text: M = T(xf, yf) × S(sx, sy) × T(-xf, -yf)

g.data-narration: Scale line AB from A(2,4),B(6,8) by 0.5 about F(4,6). After three steps: A'(3,5), B'(5,7), F stays at (4,6)!
g.text: Example: Scale Line by 0.5 about Fixed Point F(4,6)

g.data-narration: Multiple transformations combine into one matrix via multiplication. Remember: right-to-left application! M = T × S means S first, then T.
g.text: Combine transformations by matrix multiplication: M = T × S
CRITICAL: Matrix multiplication is NOT commutative! Order matters!
Transformations apply RIGHT-TO-LEFT: M = T × S applies S first, then T

g.data-narration: Summary of key formulas: Translation matrix, Scaling matrix, Fixed point scaling formula, and transformation equations.
Remember: translation is commutative but scaling is not. Negative scale factors cause reflection. Practice matrix derivation step by step!
g.text: • Remember: right-to-left transformation application | w=1 for homogeneous coords
• Fixed point scaling: translate→scale→translate back | Negative sx/sy = reflection
• Translation preserves shape; scaling changes size | Always verify with test points
